title: HP Inc.: The Subscription Model Is The Future
text: HP Inc. is considered a resilient business model with potential for growth in the context of interest hikes and a potential recession scenario. HPQ's transition to a subscription model, starting with its printing business, is attracting investors like Warren Buffett. The company's combination of metrics, including strong return on equity and lower stock-based compensation, makes it an attractive investment compared to its competitors in the PC industry.

title: Why HP Stock Fell on Wednesday
text: HP's revenue fell 9.9% year over year in its fiscal third quarter. The company is gaining share in a weak PC market.

title: HP dampens profit expectations on sluggish PC and China demand
text: HP Inc (NYSE:HPQ) shares slid 8% to $28.90 in late-morning trading on Wednesday after the information technology company posted mixed third-quarter 2023 financial results and issued softer-than-expected 4Q guidance.  Inflation and global economic uncertainty has sparked a more than year-long slump in personal computer (PC) sales and has led to increased inventory across the supply chain.

title: A Revenue Rebound Could Be Due For HP
text: HP Inc. could experience a rebound in revenues. As the company's Wall Street analysts don't expect such a move, this could surprise the market positively in the near-to-medium term. The company has steady revenues and impressively stable operating margins in its history. At the current price, HP's stock is quite modestly priced. With the possible rebound, I believe the stock is worthy of a buy rating.

title: HP Inc. to Announce Third Quarter Fiscal 2023 Earnings on August 29, 2023

title: HP Inc: Debt Clouds Dividend Safety Amidst Slow Recovery
text: HP Inc. has declared its quarterly dividend of 26.25 cents, marking the 4th consecutive quarter with the same dividend, indicating a likely annual dividend increase in Q4 2023. HP's dividend growth history shows consistent increases since 2015, and the company's strong dividend coverage suggests it can afford a further increase. Dividend coverage seem strong on commonly used metrics but diminishes when debt load is considered.
